event: The Mystical Marriage of Saint Catherine of Alexandria
symbols: ring placed on Catherine's finger — the mystical marriage, spiritual union between the saint and Christ
enthroned Madonna and Child — the divine court before which the mystical marriage takes place
John the Baptist pointing — the forerunner directing attention to Christ the Bridegroom
ornate carpet and throne — the heavenly court of the King of Kings
Catherine kneeling — devotion and surrender to Christ as the soul's bridegroom
description: A richly detailed central panel showing the Virgin Mary enthroned with the Christ child on her lap, beneath an ornate Gothic canopy. The child reaches toward a kneeling woman in elegant dress (Catherine) at right, appearing to place a ring on her finger. At the far left, a red-robed male figure stands. At the far right, a figure in animal skins (John the Baptist) stands. An angel is visible in the background. The floor is covered with an elaborate Oriental carpet. The painting is rich in color and meticulous in detail, characteristic of Flemish art.
theological_description: Memling's central panel depicts the Mystical Marriage of Saint Catherine of Alexandria — a vision in which the Christ child places a ring on Catherine's finger, symbolizing her total spiritual devotion to Christ as the Bridegroom of the soul. This mystical marriage tradition draws on the rich biblical imagery of the divine Bridegroom. The Song of Solomon celebrates the love between lover and beloved, interpreted by both Jewish and Christian traditions as the love between God and his people: 'My beloved is mine and I am his' (Song of Solomon 2:16). Paul explicitly uses the marriage metaphor: 'I promised you to one husband, to Christ, so that I might present you as a pure virgin to him' (2 Corinthians 11:2). The Book of Revelation culminates in the marriage supper of the Lamb: 'Let us rejoice and be glad and give him glory! For the wedding of the Lamb has come, and his bride has made herself ready' (Revelation 19:7). John the Baptist's presence is deeply fitting — he himself called Jesus 'the bridegroom' and declared: 'The bride belongs to the bridegroom. The friend who attends the bridegroom waits and listens for him, and is full of joy when he hears the bridegroom's voice' (John 3:29). Catherine's mystical marriage thus becomes an individual anticipation of the cosmic marriage between Christ and his Church.

scripture_refs.reason: The wedding of the Lamb — the eschatological marriage that Catherine's mystical union anticipates
Paul presenting believers as a pure virgin to Christ — the theological basis for mystical marriage
My beloved is mine and I am his — the Song of Solomon's bridal imagery
The Baptist identifying Jesus as the Bridegroom — John stands witness in the painting
Christ loving the Church as a husband loves his wife — the ecclesial dimension of the mystical marriage

_meta.title: Triptych of the Mystical Marriage of St. Catherine of Alexandria
